articles.title: Neuroimmune abnormalities may play a key role in fibromyalgia
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers from the University of Barcelona report that neuroimmune alterations in the central nervous system may contribute to fibromyalgia. The article frames the finding as a possible clue to disease development and progression rather than a definitive explanation. It highlights the condition's biological complexity and the need for further mechanistic work.

articles.title: Clues to psilocybin's epigenetic effects on people with alcohol use disorder
articles.summary_3_sentences: This report describes research into psilocybin's epigenetic effects in people with alcohol use disorder. It positions the work within growing interest in psychedelics as potential mental health treatments. The article emphasizes biological clues rather than proven clinical benefit.

articles.title: How dopamine modulates brain circuits of motivation in a behavioral addiction model
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers at Kanazawa University identified dopamine-related mechanisms of motivation in a mouse model of behavioral addiction. The findings are preclinical and focus on neural circuitry rather than human treatment outcomes. The study may help refine future addiction research targets.

articles.title: Three genes may link six mental disorders through shared biomarkers
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article reports that three genes may connect six neuropsychiatric and neurodevelopmental disorders through shared biomarkers. It suggests that conditions such as schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, ADHD, and autism could have overlapping biological signals. The work is framed as a step toward understanding common mechanisms, not collapsing distinct diagnoses into one.

articles.title: Tubulin prevents toxic brain protein clumps linked to Alzheimer's and Parkinson's
articles.summary_3_sentences: Scientists reported that tubulin can redirect tau and alpha-synuclein away from forming toxic aggregates inside brain cells. The work points to a cellular mechanism relevant to both Alzheimer's and Parkinson's disease. It is mechanistic research and does not yet establish a therapy in people.

articles.title: Scientists discover neurons must break their DNA to build the brain
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found that migrating newborn neurons in the developing brain can sustain double-strand DNA breaks as they move through tight spaces. The study also showed that the young brain rapidly repairs this damage. The findings offer a basic-science view of how brain development balances physical stress with genome maintenance.

articles.title: Scientists reprogram brain immune cells to fight Alzheimer's
articles.summary_3_sentences: A molecule called OLE reportedly shifted brain immune cells toward a more protective state in Alzheimer's models. In these models, treatment reduced plaque burden and improved memory-related outcomes. The results are preclinical and suggest a possible therapeutic direction rather than a demonstrated human treatment.

articles.title: Scientists May Have Found What Really Triggers Alzheimer's Disease
articles.summary_3_sentences: This report says amyloid beta may trigger Alzheimer's pathology by interfering with tau's normal neuronal role. The study reframes disease initiation around interactions between two hallmark proteins rather than plaques alone. It presents a mechanistic hypothesis, not a final resolution of Alzheimer's causation.

articles.title: A daily probiotic may help relieve depression and anxiety
articles.summary_3_sentences: A small clinical trial found that older adults with depression who took a daily probiotic alongside antidepressants had slightly greater improvements in depression and anxiety than those given placebo. The findings suggest adjunctive benefit but remain preliminary. Larger studies are needed to confirm effect size and durability.

articles.title: 10 surprising ways diabetes and dementia are connected
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article reviews multiple pathways linking diabetes and dementia, including glucose regulation, vascular damage, and inflammation. It also notes emerging evidence that some diabetes medicines may influence dementia risk. The piece is explanatory and synthesizes research rather than reporting one new intervention trial.

articles.title: Scientists found an early depression clue hidden in children's eyes
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found that children's attention to emotional facial expressions differed by inherited depression risk. Higher-risk children became more focused on sadness, while lower-risk children showed reduced attention to happiness. The study points to a possible early cognitive marker, not a diagnostic test.

articles.title: Copper drug clears toxic Alzheimer's proteins and restores memory
articles.summary_3_sentences: Laboratory experiments found that a copper-based compound improved clearance of toxic Alzheimer's-related proteins and improved memory measures. Because the drug has already been tested in humans for other neurological uses, the report suggests possible repurposing potential. The current evidence described here remains preclinical.

articles.title: This popular brain supplement was linked to shorter lifespans in men
articles.summary_3_sentences: A large-scale study linked higher tyrosine levels in men to shorter lifespan. The report is notable because tyrosine is commonly marketed in brain-health supplements. It identifies an association and does not prove that supplementation itself causes reduced survival.

articles.title: Your brain was never designed for this much bad news
articles.summary_3_sentences: This piece explains how human attention systems are tuned to threat and can be strained by constant exposure to negative news. Researchers recommend healthier habits around media consumption rather than disengagement from current events altogether. It is a behavioral-science perspective on mental well-being in modern information environments.

articles.title: Your brain can keep improving into your 90s, study finds
articles.summary_3_sentences: A three-year study of nearly 4,000 adults found measurable gains in several aspects of brain health across a wide age range, including into the 90s. Participants used brief daily brain-training activities. The findings challenge assumptions of inevitable late-life decline, though the article does not claim that all domains improve equally for everyone.

articles.title: A hidden gene finally explains this rare neurological disorder
articles.summary_3_sentences: After analyzing nearly 3,000 patients, researchers identified CD99L2 mutations as a cause of a rare movement disorder. The gene had previously been linked mainly to immune function, and the study suggests it is also important for nerve-cell communication. The finding may improve molecular diagnosis of unexplained neurological disease.

articles.title: Scientists mapped every neural connection in a fruit fly and find a surprise
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers produced a complete connectome of an adult fruit fly central nervous system. The map suggests complex behaviors may arise from distributed local circuits rather than a single command center. This is foundational neuroscience with potential implications for understanding how brains organize movement and behavior.

articles.title: Popular joint supplement glucosamine linked to faster Alzheimer's progression
articles.summary_3_sentences: A study linked glucosamine use to a higher likelihood of progression from mild cognitive impairment to Alzheimer's disease. Researchers also reported biological clues that might explain the association. The report is observational in framing and does not establish that glucosamine directly accelerates dementia.

articles.title: Scientists found a new Alzheimer's trigger and a drug that stops it
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers identified a new Alzheimer's-related target and developed an experimental compound to block the damaging process in mice. In preclinical testing, the treatment reduced neurodegenerative changes and appeared to support healthier aging. The findings are early-stage and not yet evidence of clinical efficacy in humans.

articles.title: Learning a musical instrument in your 70s could help protect memory
articles.summary_3_sentences: In a four-year study, older adults who kept practicing a musical instrument maintained memory performance better than those who stopped. They also showed less age-related brain shrinkage in regions related to memory and learning. The findings support continued skill learning as a possible contributor to healthier cognitive aging.

articles.title: Scientists reverse anxiety by fixing a tiny brain circuit
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers identified a small group of amygdala neurons involved in anxiety and social behavior in mice. Restoring activity in this circuit reversed anxiety-like and social deficits in the animal model. The work is preclinical and highlights a possible target for future therapies.

articles.title: Brain scans reveal two distinct types of autism
articles.summary_3_sentences: By combining human brain imaging with data from genetically engineered mouse models, researchers identified two biological connectivity patterns within autism. One subtype showed hyperconnectivity and the other hypoconnectivity. The findings support the idea that autism includes multiple neurobiological pathways rather than one uniform brain pattern.

articles.title: Scientists discover gut bacteria that may help protect against autism and ADHD
articles.summary_3_sentences: This study suggests that gene-related epigenetic patterns at birth may shape infant gut microbiome development and later neurodevelopmental signals. Certain combinations were linked to early markers associated with autism and ADHD by age three. The work is associative and exploratory, not a demonstrated preventive strategy.

articles.title: Scientists found the hidden switch fueling alzheimer's brain inflammation
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers reported that chemical modification of the protein STING may keep the brain's immune system activated in Alzheimer's disease. The mechanism was linked to damage at neuronal connections. The finding adds to growing evidence that neuroinflammation is a central process in disease progression.

articles.title: Protein traffic jams may explain aging, memory loss, and Alzheimer's
articles.summary_3_sentences: Studying turquoise killifish, scientists found that ribosomes can increasingly collide and stall with age, disrupting protein production. The resulting buildup of faulty proteins may help explain memory decline and neurodegenerative vulnerability. The work is mechanistic and based on an animal model of aging.

articles.title: Human organoids reveal how to reverse "irreversible" nerve damage
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers used human brain-and-spinal-cord organoids to study why neurons lose regenerative capacity during development. They identified a gene network involved in this shift and reported that an existing hormone drug boosted nerve fiber regrowth in the lab system. The work is preclinical but offers a platform for testing regenerative strategies.

articles.title: New depression treatment targets the immune system instead of the brain
articles.summary_3_sentences: A small clinical trial found that an anti-inflammatory drug commonly used for rheumatoid arthritis showed early promise in hard-to-treat depression. Participants also had improvements in fatigue, anxiety, and quality of life. The results are preliminary and support further investigation of immune-targeted approaches in psychiatry.

articles.title: Researchers block key protein that helps Parkinson's spread through the brain
articles.summary_3_sentences: Scientists identified GPNMB as a protein that may help Parkinson's-related pathology spread between cells. In early experiments, blocking the protein with antibodies interrupted this toxic process. The research is preclinical and points to a possible disease-modifying target.

articles.title: Scientists thought brain inflammation was driving long COVID but the scans told a different story
articles.summary_3_sentences: A brain imaging study found no evidence of widespread neuroinflammation in patients with long COVID. Instead, more severe symptoms were associated with increased activity in mood- and emotion-related regions. The findings may shift attention toward altered brain function rather than diffuse inflammation alone.

articles.title: Scientists create supercharged vitamin K that helps the brain heal itself
articles.summary_3_sentences: Japanese researchers developed vitamin K-derived compounds that more effectively pushed neural stem cells toward becoming neurons. The article suggests possible relevance to regenerative approaches for Alzheimer's and Parkinson's disease. This remains early laboratory-stage work.

articles.title: Popular anti-aging drug combo caused severe brain damage in mice
articles.summary_3_sentences: Mouse research found that a drug combination explored for anti-aging caused severe myelin loss and brain changes resembling chemo brain. Some of the damaged cells also resembled those seen in multiple sclerosis. The results underscore the risks of translating anti-aging strategies without careful neurological evaluation.

articles.title: USC scientists discover a hidden Alzheimer's trigger and a possible way to shut it down
articles.summary_3_sentences: USC researchers identified compounds that may reduce Alzheimer's-related brain inflammation by targeting cPLA2, especially in the context of APOE4. The study points to a specific enzyme as both a risk-linked mechanism and a drug target. It is a preclinical therapeutic lead rather than a proven treatment.

articles.title: Caffeine reversed memory problems caused by sleep deprivation
articles.summary_3_sentences: Laboratory research found that sleep deprivation disrupted a brain circuit involved in social memory, and caffeine restored signaling in that pathway. The effect was described as relatively targeted in the animal experiments. The findings add mechanistic detail to how lost sleep can impair cognition.

articles.title: Cervical Cancer Mortality Rate Sees a Sharp Decline Following HPV Vaccination Usage
articles.summary_3_sentences: This report highlights Lancet findings showing a near-zero risk of cervical cancer death among young women after HPV vaccination. The article frames the decline as a major public health success. It supports the population-level impact of vaccination on cancer mortality.

articles.title: Only a third of eligible breast cancer patients receive a gene expression test
articles.summary_3_sentences: New research from the Netherlands found that only about one in three eligible breast cancer patients receive a gene expression test that can help avoid unnecessary chemotherapy. Uptake remained limited even after reimbursement began. The article points to an implementation gap between evidence-based tools and routine care.

articles.title: Mass spectrometry platform could shrink cancer drug discovery cycle to four hours
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers at Purdue describe a mass spectrometry platform intended to speed up early-stage cancer drug discovery. The technology targets one of the slowest steps in identifying promising compounds. It is a translational tools story rather than a report of a new approved therapy.

articles.title: This DNA repair gene went rogue and exposed a cancer weakness
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found that overexpression of the DNA repair gene EXO1 can itself create harmful DNA damage in cancer cells. The work reveals how a normally protective pathway may become a vulnerability when dysregulated. It could inform strategies that exploit DNA repair imbalance in tumors.

articles.title: Combination Therapy Shows Promise for Hormone-Receptor Positive Breast Cancer
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reports on combination therapy showing promise in hormone-receptor positive breast cancer. Based on the limited summary, it presents the treatment as a potentially beneficial approach in a common breast cancer subtype. Specific efficacy details are not provided in the record.

articles.title: Proteins Linked to High-Risk Lung Cancer in time for Adequate Prevention
articles.summary_3_sentences: The report says certain proteins were linked to high-risk lung cancer, with implications for prevention. It appears to focus on biomarker identification rather than a direct treatment advance. The available summary is limited, so the article is best read as an early risk-stratification story.

articles.title: Scientists discover a surprising cancer link to Alzheimer's disease
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found that blood-cancer-related mutations may contribute to Alzheimer's disease by generating highly inflammatory brain immune cells. The article suggests a connection between clonal hematopoiesis and neurodegeneration. It may open paths for blood-based screening or cross-disciplinary therapeutic ideas.

articles.title: Scientists shut down cancer DNA repair to overcome drug resistance
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers reported that UNI418 can impair cancer cells' ability to repair DNA damage. When paired with a PARP inhibitor, it restored sensitivity in resistant cancer cells. The work points to a potential strategy for overcoming treatment resistance in tumors that rely on DNA repair.

articles.title: GLP-1 Drugs Linked to 35% Lower Risk of Breast Cancer
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reports that women taking GLP-1 drugs were less likely to develop breast cancer in a study dataset. The result is presented as an association rather than proof of prevention. It adds to interest in the broader effects of obesity and diabetes drugs on cancer risk.

articles.title: The secret reason some cancer treatments stop working
articles.summary_3_sentences: Scientists identified SLAMF6 as an immune brake that may weaken tumor-fighting T cells and contribute to treatment failure. In mice, antibodies that blocked the molecule helped immune cells remain more effective. The findings are preclinical and suggest a possible new immunotherapy target.

articles.title: Cancer patients found a simple way to stay mentally sharp during chemotherapy
articles.summary_3_sentences: A trial found that cancer patients who followed a home-based exercise program had better attention and fewer cognitive complaints during chemotherapy. Low-dose ibuprofen improved some measures but less consistently. The study suggests supportive care strategies may help manage chemo-related cognitive symptoms.

articles.title: Combination Chemotherapy and Immunotherapy Treatment for Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer
articles.summary_3_sentences: This piece covers a combined chemotherapy and immunotherapy approach for non-small cell lung cancer. The limited summary indicates a treatment-focused oncology report without detailed outcomes in the record. It reflects continued interest in combination regimens for NSCLC.

articles.title: Ozempic and similar weight-loss drugs linked to 30% lower breast cancer risk
articles.summary_3_sentences: A large study found that women taking GLP-1 drugs had a lower incidence of breast cancer. Researchers emphasized that the findings are promising but not proof of causation, and clinical trials are being planned. The report suggests a possible preventive signal that requires formal testing.

articles.title: Scientists finally crack an "undruggable" pancreatic cancer target and nearly double survival
articles.summary_3_sentences: A major clinical trial found that daraxonrasib, which targets KRAS-driven pancreatic cancer, nearly doubled survival in advanced disease. The article presents the drug as a breakthrough against a target long considered difficult to drug. If confirmed and adopted, the finding could mark a substantial change in pancreatic cancer therapy.

articles.title: Melanoma Vaccine Extends Cancer-Free Survival
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reports that a melanoma vaccine extended cancer-free survival in findings presented at ASCO and published alongside the meeting. The summary indicates a recurrence-related outcome rather than cure. It reflects ongoing efforts to use vaccines as part of cancer immunotherapy.

articles.title: Why cancer spreads more in middle age than in old age
articles.summary_3_sentences: In mice, melanoma spread was lowest in the young, highest in middle age, and lower again in very old animals. Researchers linked this pattern to an immune cell population that helps keep cancer dormant. The findings are preclinical and suggest age-related metastasis risk may not follow a simple linear pattern.

articles.title: Scientists discover why some DNA-doubled cells refuse to die
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers examined what happens when cells duplicate DNA but fail to divide, a state linked to cancer and aging. They found that not all such cells behave the same, implying hidden biological differences in survival after division errors. The study may inform how abnormal cell states contribute to tumor evolution.

articles.title: Ozempic changed obesity treatment, but experts say the real revolution is next
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reviews how GLP-1 drugs transformed obesity care and argues that the next phase will be more personalized and multimodal. Experts envision combinations of medication, procedures, surgery, and precision medicine. It is a forward-looking analysis rather than a single study result.

articles.title: Ozempic and Wegovy linked to surprising drop in violent behavior
articles.summary_3_sentences: A Rutgers study found that GLP-1 drug use was associated with a weaker link between impulsivity and violent behavior. Researchers emphasized that the finding does not prove causation. The result adds to emerging interest in behavioral effects of this drug class beyond weight and glucose control.

articles.title: Most people who stop GLP-1 drugs like Ozempic eventually return
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found that many patients discontinue GLP-1 medications but a substantial portion later restart treatment. Newer agents appeared to support longer persistence, while side effects remained a common reason for stopping. The article highlights real-world adherence patterns in diabetes care.

articles.title: Semaglutide (Ozempic) linked to fewer bone fractures despite greater weight loss
articles.summary_3_sentences: A large real-world study of nearly 60,000 adults with type 2 diabetes found fewer fractures among people taking semaglutide than among those using other weight-loss medications. The finding is notable because weight loss can sometimes raise concern about bone health. It is observational evidence and does not by itself prove a protective drug effect.

articles.title: Semaglutide Linked to Lower Risk of Bone Fracture
articles.summary_3_sentences: This Labroots report covers research linking semaglutide with lower bone fracture risk in people with type 2 diabetes. The article presents the drug as potentially protective despite ongoing weight loss. The record gives limited detail beyond the central association.

articles.title: New GLP-1 diabetes pill delivers major weight loss and blood sugar control
articles.summary_3_sentences: A major clinical trial found that a new oral GLP-1 medication improved blood sugar control and produced substantial weight loss in type 2 diabetes. The article suggests the results could expand access through a pill form rather than injections. It describes the treatment as a promising convenience advance within an already effective drug class.

articles.title: People taking GLP-1 weight loss drugs like Ozempic started moving less
articles.summary_3_sentences: Fitbit-based research found lower daily steps and exercise after people began GLP-1 weight-loss medications. The article raises concern that reduced physical activity, together with muscle loss, could affect long-term health even as weight decreases. It points to the importance of exercise support alongside drug treatment.

articles.title: Reversing prediabetes cuts risk of deadly heart problems by 58%
articles.summary_3_sentences: Long-term studies in the U.S. and China found that people who returned from prediabetes to normal glucose levels had markedly lower risks of cardiovascular death, heart failure hospitalization, and major cardiovascular events. The article underscores that prediabetes is modifiable and clinically meaningful. It links metabolic improvement with durable cardiovascular benefit.

articles.title: Popular GLP-1 weight-loss drugs linked to lower risks of addiction and overdose
articles.summary_3_sentences: A large study of U.S. veterans found that GLP-1 drugs were associated with lower risks of multiple substance use disorders, overdoses, hospitalizations, and drug-related deaths. The findings suggest these medications may influence reward-related behaviors as well as metabolism. Because the study is observational, it does not prove a direct anti-addiction effect.

articles.title: This new diabetes pill burns fat without the downsides of Ozempic
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers developed an experimental pill that works through skeletal muscle metabolism rather than appetite suppression. Early clinical results suggest it lowers blood sugar and increases fat burning while preserving muscle mass. The article presents it as a potential alternative pathway for diabetes and obesity treatment.

articles.title: Scientists discover why ozempic may not work for some people
articles.summary_3_sentences: Scientists identified genetic variants associated with reduced response to GLP-1 medications in type 2 diabetes. About 10% of the population may carry this apparent GLP-1 resistance pattern. The finding could eventually support more personalized prescribing and expectations for treatment response.

articles.title: Scientists discover why Ozempic and Wegovy weight loss eventually plateaus
articles.summary_3_sentences: NIH researchers reported that semaglutide triggers different responses in appetite-regulating brain cells, which may help explain treatment plateaus. They also describe a possible way to prolong effects. The study offers mechanistic insight into variable and time-limited weight-loss response.

articles.title: AI scans 400,000 Reddit posts and finds hidden Ozempic side effects
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers used AI to analyze social media posts and identify frequently discussed symptoms among GLP-1 drug users, including menstrual irregularities, chills, and hot flashes. The article suggests this kind of monitoring could complement traditional pharmacovigilance. It highlights signal detection rather than confirmed causal side effects.

articles.title: Wegovy linked to rare "eye stroke" that can cause sudden blindness
articles.summary_3_sentences: An analysis of FDA adverse-event reports found a stronger safety signal for a rare optic event with Wegovy than with Ozempic. The article raises concern about sudden vision loss risk while noting both drugs contain semaglutide. As with all spontaneous-report analyses, the finding is a signal rather than proof of causation or incidence.

articles.title: Single amino acid change may help viruses jump from bat to human
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reports that a single amino acid change may help some viruses adapt from bats to humans. The finding highlights how small molecular shifts can alter host range. It is mechanistic work relevant to zoonotic spillover risk.

articles.title: Study Found Low Likelihood of Fentanyl Added to Cannabis Sold on the Black Market
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers analyzing illicit-market cannabis found a low likelihood of fentanyl contamination. The article suggests the specific risk may be less common than some public fears imply. It reports on contamination surveillance rather than endorsing black-market use as safe.

articles.title: Major review finds vaping likely causes lung and oral cancer
articles.summary_3_sentences: A major review concluded that nicotine vaping likely contributes to lung and oral cancers, drawing on human biomarkers, animal data, and lab studies. The article challenges claims that vaping is harmless. It presents risk evidence but not a quantified population incidence estimate.

articles.title: Ancient DNA reveals plague was already killing humans 5,500 years ago
articles.summary_3_sentences: Ancient DNA analysis from Siberian hunter-gatherer cemeteries found early plague strains in a large share of individuals studied. Researchers inferred that family-based outbreaks may have caused substantial mortality thousands of years before classic urban plague settings. The article adds evolutionary context to the history of infectious disease.

articles.title: One Amino Acid Switch Can Move a Virus from Bats to Humans
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article also covers research suggesting a single amino acid change can enable viral host shifts from bats to humans. The available summary is brief but clearly ties molecular change to zoonotic potential. It is a basic-science finding relevant to pandemic emergence research.

articles.title: New study explores potential cross-species spread of chronic wasting disease
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found that chronic wasting disease prions can be present even in animals without symptoms, indicating silent spread. Although no human transmission has been confirmed, the article says the disease's capacity to evolve across species deserves close monitoring. It is a risk-assessment and surveillance-oriented finding.

articles.title: New fentanyl vaccine blocks deadly overdoses before they start
articles.summary_3_sentences: Scripps researchers developed an experimental vaccine designed to block fentanyl and related synthetic opioids before they reach the brain. The vaccine targets a range of fentanyl-like compounds rather than one molecule alone. It remains experimental but could represent a new overdose-prevention approach.

articles.title: Proteins Help Deadly Pathogen Evade Immunity
articles.summary_3_sentences: This report describes proteins that help the bacterium causing louse-borne relapsing fever evade immune defenses. The record suggests a mechanistic study of pathogen survival. Specific experimental details are limited in the summary provided.

articles.title: The deadly tapeworm spreading across America has reached the Pacific Northwest
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers detected Echinococcus multilocularis in a high proportion of coyotes around Puget Sound, marking its emergence in the Pacific Northwest. The parasite can cause severe disease in humans and animals. The article emphasizes wildlife surveillance and geographic spread rather than documented human outbreak numbers.

articles.title: A COVID-19 Preventive Strategy is Now FDA Approved
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reports FDA approval of a COVID-19 preventive strategy. The limited summary indicates a regulatory and public health development rather than a mechanistic study. Specific product details are not available in the provided record.

articles.title: AI-designed universal coronavirus vaccine passes first human trial
articles.summary_3_sentences: An AI-designed universal coronavirus vaccine was reported safe and well tolerated in its first human trial. It generated immune responses across multiple coronaviruses, including SARS-CoV-2, SARS, and related bat viruses. The strategy aims to protect against virus families rather than single strains.

articles.title: Specific Immune Cells Found to Target a Range of Viral Strains
articles.summary_3_sentences: This report says specific T cells were identified that can target multiple viral strains. The finding points to breadth in immune recognition that may matter for vaccine or antiviral design. The summary is limited but clearly places the work in basic immunology.

articles.title: A Gut Microbe Can Modulate the Severity of Sepsis
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article reports that a gut microbe can influence how severe sepsis becomes. It suggests the microbiome may shape host immune control during overwhelming infection. The provided summary is brief and does not specify model system or mechanism.

articles.title: Smoking restrictions gain support across all 50 states over 30 years
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers developed and validated a way to measure changing social norms around smoking and secondhand smoke across the United States over three decades. The article reports broad growth in support for smoking restrictions in all 50 states. It is a public health norms and policy story with relevance to tobacco control.

articles.title: Most people seeking a kidney transplant in the US never reach the waitlist
articles.summary_3_sentences: A new study found that many people referred for kidney transplantation never complete the steps needed to join the waitlist. Less than one-fifth reportedly finish the assessment process. The article highlights a major access bottleneck before formal organ allocation even begins.

articles.title: Think you're eating healthy? You may be missing this heart-protecting nutrient
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article argues that fruit and vegetable intake alone may not ensure adequate flavanol consumption for cardiovascular health. It identifies foods such as apples, blackberries, cherries, broad beans, and green tea as richer sources. The story is nutrition-focused and emphasizes food choice within broadly healthy diets.

articles.title: Eight Common Food Additives Linked to Heart Disease
articles.summary_3_sentences: This report describes a study linking several common food preservatives to higher cardiovascular risk. The article raises concern about routine exposure to additives in processed foods. The record provides limited methodological detail, so the finding should be read as an association summary.

articles.title: A surprising discovery reveals the kidney has a secret backup system
articles.summary_3_sentences: Mayo Clinic researchers described a previously unrecognized kidney water-conservation pathway that appears to operate independently of the classic controlling hormone. The discovery emerged unexpectedly during work on polycystic kidney disease and probenecid. It is a physiology finding with possible implications for kidney disorders and fluid balance.

articles.title: Sleep apnea's hidden heart disease trigger found in the gut
articles.summary_3_sentences: In mice, researchers found that blocking the bile acid receptor FXR sharply reduced plaque buildup associated with sleep apnea-related cardiovascular risk. The study points to a gut-heart signaling pathway involving microbial metabolites. It is preclinical work that may guide future therapeutic research.

articles.title: New drug could finally stop deadly fatty liver disease
articles.summary_3_sentences: An experimental drug, ION224, was reported to improve liver health in clinical trials for MASH by targeting a liver enzyme involved in fat accumulation and inflammation. The article notes benefits even without weight loss. It suggests a potentially important advance for a disease with limited treatment options.

articles.title: Common heart drug taken by millions found useless -- and possibly dangerous
articles.summary_3_sentences: A large international study found no benefit from beta blockers after uncomplicated heart attacks in patients with preserved heart function. The article also reports worse outcomes among women taking the drugs in this setting. If validated in guidelines, the finding could alter routine post-heart-attack prescribing.

articles.title: Beet juice lowers blood pressure in older adults in just 2 weeks
articles.summary_3_sentences: In older adults, nitrate-rich beetroot juice lowered blood pressure over two weeks and appeared to alter oral bacteria in a favorable way. Younger adults did not show the same effect. The article links diet, microbiome shifts, and vascular outcomes.

articles.title: As patients move through injury care, well-intentioned changes can backfire
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers argue that improving one part of injury care does not always improve outcomes if the rest of the health system is not prepared. The article emphasizes coordination across services rather than isolated reforms. It is a systems-oriented global health perspective on trauma care.

articles.title: Medical Cannabis May Offer A Safer and More Effective Treatment for Hypermobility Patients Than Opioids
articles.summary_3_sentences: A study in Clinical Rheumatology focused on people with Ehlers-Danlos syndrome and hypermobility and suggested medical cannabis may compare favorably with opioids for pain management. The article presents it as a potentially safer and more effective option. The summary does not provide detailed trial design or magnitude of benefit.

articles.title: Scientists found a cannabis compound that relieves pain without the high
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found that cannabis-derived terpenes reduced pain in mouse models of fibromyalgia and post-surgical pain. One terpene, geraniol, showed especially strong effects, and the compounds are not associated with THC-like psychoactive effects. The work is preclinical and does not yet show pain relief in humans.

articles.title: Inflammation Worsens Brain Damage
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reports that inflammation can worsen the severity of traumatic brain injury-related damage. It frames immune activation as an important contributor to long-term cognitive consequences after blows to the head. The summary is brief and does not detail the model or intervention.

articles.title: New procedure delivers lasting knee arthritis pain relief without surgery
articles.summary_3_sentences: A minimally invasive procedure that blocks inflammation-driving blood vessels in the knee improved pain and function in osteoarthritis patients for at least a year. The article describes the treatment as safe and potentially useful before knee replacement. It represents a procedural alternative rather than a drug therapy.

articles.title: Calcium and Vitamin D Don't Prevent Bone Fractures
articles.summary_3_sentences: This report states that calcium and vitamin D, alone or together, do not protect older adults against fractures or falls. The article aligns with a broader reassessment of routine supplementation for bone protection. Details are limited, but the conclusion is clear in the provided summary.

articles.title: Millions take calcium and vitamin D for stronger bones. A major review finds little benefit
articles.summary_3_sentences: A review of nearly 154,000 people found little meaningful fracture or fall prevention from calcium, vitamin D, or both in most older adults. The article challenges longstanding assumptions about routine supplementation. It may influence clinical conversations about when these supplements are actually indicated.

articles.title: Drug Delays Arthritis by up to 4 Years in High-Risk Patients
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reports that abatacept delayed the onset of rheumatoid arthritis in high-risk patients for up to four years. It presents the result as evidence that progression to clinical arthritis may be preventable or postponable. The summary gives limited trial detail but conveys a meaningful preventive effect.

articles.title: This drug delayed rheumatoid arthritis for years after treatment ended
articles.summary_3_sentences: A study found that one year of abatacept treatment delayed rheumatoid arthritis onset by up to four years in high-risk individuals. Benefits persisted after treatment stopped. The article suggests immune intervention may modify the course before full disease develops.

articles.title: Scientists "recharge" damaged nerves to ease chronic pain
articles.summary_3_sentences: Duke researchers found that supplying damaged nerves with healthy mitochondria reduced chronic nerve pain in laboratory studies. The approach targets nerve energy failure rather than masking symptoms. It is an early-stage mechanistic and therapeutic concept, not a clinical treatment yet.

articles.title: Down syndrome isn't a tragedy, but misinformation about it is
articles.summary_3_sentences: This commentary-style news piece argues that misinformation and deficit-based framing around Down syndrome shape prenatal counseling, family decisions, and social attitudes. It links those narratives to harms such as exclusion and institutionalization. The article is centered on disability, ethics, and communication in medicine.

articles.title: How woodchips can help keep ticks off trails
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article discusses woodchips as a practical trail-management strategy to reduce tick exposure. It is framed as a seasonal public health measure for outdoor settings. The record gives limited detail but clearly focuses on prevention of tick encounters.

articles.title: How co-parenting reduces overall parental stress
articles.summary_3_sentences: A University of Delaware researcher describes how co-parenting and broader social support can reduce parental stress. The article presents this as both a research and practice insight. It emphasizes family and community structure rather than a medical intervention.

articles.title: The over‑50s are most likely to overdose. Here's how older people use drugs
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article highlights rising overdose deaths among older Australians, challenging stereotypes that overdose is mainly a problem of youth. It focuses on how older adults use drugs and the public health implications of an aging overdose burden. The piece is educational and population-focused.

articles.title: What drives women to have a 'freebirth' without a midwife or doctor? Here's what the research says
articles.summary_3_sentences: Prompted by a coronial inquest, this article reviews research on why some women choose freebirth without clinical attendance. It frames the issue around trust, maternity care experiences, and perceived autonomy. The piece is primarily a public health and maternal care analysis.

articles.title: Even Compliant Cannabis Packaging Can Appeal to Teens
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found that cannabis edible packaging can appeal to teenagers even when it meets legal compliance standards. The resemblance to familiar snacks and health foods was cited as one reason. The article has implications for youth protection and product regulation.

articles.title: Why middle age is becoming a breaking point in the U.S.
articles.summary_3_sentences: An international study found that middle-aged Americans are experiencing worsening loneliness, depression, memory, and general health relative to earlier generations. Researchers point to chronic stress, weaker social supports, and financial strain. The article presents a broad social determinants perspective on health decline.

articles.title: Why grandparents matter more than ever for children's mental health
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article argues that grandparents can play an important protective role in children's mental health by offering support, listening, and meaning. It frames rising youth mental health needs as a family and community challenge. The piece is interpretive and practical rather than reporting a single trial.

articles.title: National Cancer Survivors Day Celebrates Survivorship
articles.summary_3_sentences: This item marks National Cancer Survivors Day and recognizes people living with a history of cancer. It is an awareness and observance article rather than a research report. The piece focuses on survivorship and public recognition.

articles.title: Frozen fruit and canned veg are cheap, but are they as healthy as fresh food?
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article discusses whether frozen fruit and canned vegetables can offer nutrition comparable to fresh produce. It addresses affordability and practical food choices rather than promoting one form exclusively. The piece is consumer-oriented nutrition guidance.

articles.title: Long-lived families reveal a rare genetic clue to healthy aging
articles.summary_3_sentences: A study of long-lived families identified rare genetic variants associated with healthier aging, including one tied to lower inflammation. The article suggests these variants may help delay disease and extend years of good health. It is a genetics-of-longevity finding, not an intervention study.

articles.title: Yale study finds nearly half of older adults improved with age
articles.summary_3_sentences: A long-term Yale study found that nearly half of adults over 65 improved physically, mentally, or both over time. More positive views of aging were associated with greater likelihood of improvement. The article challenges assumptions that aging is only decline.

articles.title: First new US sunscreen ingredient since 1999 approved by FDA. A skin scientist explains how bemotrizinol works
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article explains the FDA approval of bemotrizinol, the first new U.S. sunscreen ingredient since 1999. It focuses on how the ingredient works in sun protection and why it matters for consumers. The piece is a science explainer tied to a regulatory development.

articles.title: Nordic walking significantly reduces depression symptoms in as little as five weeks, trial finds
articles.summary_3_sentences: A trial found that Nordic walking reduced depression symptoms within about five weeks. The article presents the activity as a full-body aerobic exercise with mental health benefits. It suggests a practical lifestyle intervention, though details on trial size and population are not given here.

articles.title: How a 45‑minute family exercise session could boost thinking skills
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article says a 45-minute family exercise session may improve thinking skills while addressing widespread inactivity. It frames physical activity as beneficial for both health and cognition. The story is practical and prevention-focused.

articles.title: 90-120 Minutes of Strength Training Per Week Increases Lifespan
articles.summary_3_sentences: This report says 90 to 120 minutes of weekly resistance training may extend lifespan. It presents moderate strength exercise as a meaningful longevity habit. The summary is brief but aligns with broader exercise epidemiology findings.

articles.title: 64% of Cannabis Consumers Say It Helps Them Sleep
articles.summary_3_sentences: A survey from the American Academy of Sleep Medicine found that 64% of cannabis consumers say cannabis helps them sleep. The report describes self-reported perceptions rather than objective sleep outcomes. It reflects user experience data, not clinical proof of effectiveness.

articles.title: Scientists found the strength training sweet spot for a longer life
articles.summary_3_sentences: A long-term study linked 90 to 120 minutes of weekly strength training to lower risks of death overall, especially from cardiovascular and neurological causes. Benefits were stronger when resistance exercise was combined with aerobic activity. The article supports moderate, regular strength work as part of longevity-focused exercise.

articles.title: Tea can improve your health and longevity, but the way you drink it matters
articles.summary_3_sentences: A major review suggests tea may support cardiovascular, metabolic, cognitive, and muscle health, but preparation and added ingredients matter. Bottled and bubble teas may not deliver the same benefit profile. The article is a nutrition synthesis rather than a new intervention trial.

articles.title: The supplements older adults actually need and the ones they don't
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reviews when supplements such as vitamin B12, vitamin D, and protein may be useful in older adults and when they may not. It emphasizes deficiency-based use over routine supplementation. The piece also warns that some supplements can interact with medications or cause harm.

articles.title: This tomato-soy juice reduced inflammation in just four weeks
articles.summary_3_sentences: In a clinical study, adults with obesity who drank a tomato-soy juice daily for four weeks had reductions in inflammatory blood markers compared with a control tomato juice. The beverage was rich in plant compounds. The article suggests a food-based approach to lowering inflammation, though longer-term outcomes remain unclear.

articles.title: Vitamin B12 and folate deficiencies linked to chronic fatigue
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers in Japan found that low vitamin B12 and folate levels may be associated with fatigue and low motivation, even in otherwise healthy people. The article points to potentially overlooked nutritional contributors to chronic tiredness. It reports an association rather than a treatment trial.

articles.title: Scientists discover hidden gut-brain circuit that triggers protein cravings
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers describe a gut-brain signaling circuit that drives animals to seek protein when amino acid supply is low. The study provides a mechanistic view of how cravings may reflect nutrient needs rather than simple preference. It is basic appetite research with potential relevance to obesity and diet design.

articles.title: Scientists say guava juice could make iron supplements work better
articles.summary_3_sentences: A review of 17 studies found that guava juice, especially alongside iron supplements, was associated with higher hemoglobin levels in women and girls. The article attributes the effect to vitamin C-enhanced iron absorption. It presents a low-cost nutritional strategy for anemia support, pending context-specific implementation.

articles.title: Surprising research reveals why you shouldn't add bananas to your smoothies
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found that bananas in berry smoothies can reduce absorption of flavanols. The article illustrates how food combinations can alter nutritional bioavailability. It is a mechanistic nutrition finding with practical dietary implications.

articles.title: CT tissue images can now be virtually stained in 3D
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers describe a method for virtually staining CT-based tissue images in three dimensions. The approach could streamline pathology-style visualization without relying only on traditional thin-section staining. It is a biomedical imaging advance with translational potential.

articles.title: Maternal weight may influence microbiota signaling in amniotic fluid
articles.summary_3_sentences: A study from Finland suggests maternal weight-related factors are reflected in microbiota-derived signaling particles in amniotic fluid. The finding raises the possibility that maternal microbiome signals influence the fetal environment. It is an early mechanistic observation rather than a clinical recommendation.

articles.title: Fragile X deficits in mice respond to gene therapy
articles.summary_3_sentences: A gene therapy restored brain activity and improved behavior in a mouse model of fragile X syndrome. The treatment was designed to replace a missing brain protein. The results are preclinical and suggest a possible path toward targeting the underlying cause rather than symptoms alone.

articles.title: Think human anatomy is finished? Scientists say think again
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article argues that human anatomy is still yielding new details and overlooked structures. It emphasizes variation between individuals and the incompleteness of current anatomical understanding. The piece is a broad science perspective rather than a single experimental report.

articles.title: DNA time stamps reveal the strawberry's surprising origins
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers used transposable-element signatures to reconstruct the complex evolutionary history of strawberries. The method revealed multiple ancient genome-merging events behind modern varieties. It is a plant genomics advance with relevance to crop evolution and breeding science.

articles.title: A Novel Way to Deliver Drugs to the Esophagus
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article describes a new approach to delivering drugs to the esophagus. The summary is truncated but clearly indicates a therapeutic delivery innovation for esophageal disorders. Specific platform and evidence details are not provided in the record.

articles.title: Novel, Protein-Chaperone Role for RNA is Revealed
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers discovered a new role for messenger RNA as a protein chaperone. The finding expands RNA biology beyond its classic function in carrying coding information. It is a basic molecular biology advance with potentially broad implications.

articles.title: Researchers Find a Cause of Inflammatory Bowel Disease
articles.summary_3_sentences: This report says scientists gained major new insights into a cause of inflammatory bowel disease. The summary indicates a mechanistic advance in gastrointestinal disease biology. Details on the pathway or model are not included in the provided record.

articles.title: Investigating Escherichia albertii - An Emerging Pathogen
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article examines Escherichia albertii as an emerging foodborne pathogen linked to serious outbreaks. It appears to be a microbiology-focused overview of an increasingly important organism. The available summary is brief but article identification is clear.

articles.title: Genetic Analysis of Cocaine Addiction Points to the Liver
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers analyzed nearly 900 rats and found genetic signals implicating the liver in cocaine addiction biology. The work suggests addiction-related mechanisms may involve peripheral organs as well as the brain. It is preclinical genetics research.

articles.title: Scientists turned red lettuce green and something surprising happened
articles.summary_3_sentences: Genome editing that blocked red pigment production in lettuce led to accumulation of other beneficial plant compounds without harming growth. The article suggests a route to tailoring crop nutrient profiles through targeted editing. It is agricultural biotechnology rather than human clinical research.

articles.title: Beneath our feet lies a fungal superhighway stretching 68 quadrillion miles
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers mapped vast underground fungal networks that move large amounts of carbon into soils and support plant life. The article frames them as a hidden planetary infrastructure with climate significance. It is environmental biology with implications for carbon cycling.

articles.title: Millipedes beat vertebrates to land by 80 million years
articles.summary_3_sentences: A new study places millipedes on land around 460 million years ago, well before vertebrates. It also clarifies their evolutionary relationships and early defenses. This is a paleobiology and evolution story rather than a medical finding.

articles.title: Genome Mapping Nearly Complete for the Pangenome
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reports major progress toward completing pangenome mapping. It frames the pangenome as a more comprehensive representation of human genetic diversity than a single reference genome. The summary is limited but the scientific focus is clear.

articles.title: Scientists discover a hidden cause of aging cells that can be reversed
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers linked age-related mitochondrial decline to falling phosphatidylcholine levels and reported reversal of dysfunction after restoring this nutrient in model systems. The article suggests some aspects of cellular aging may be modifiable. It is mechanistic aging biology rather than a ready-to-use therapy.

articles.title: A classic brain test exposed AI's biggest weakness
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers applied a classic attention task from psychology to leading AI models and found performance collapsed as task length and complexity rose. The article uses cognitive testing to probe limitations in current systems. It is a science and technology story with no direct medical claim.

articles.title: Scientists propose a radical new theory for how life began on Earth
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers propose that mineral nanoparticles acting like natural nanozymes may have helped early chemistry transition toward life. The theory emphasizes catalytic and energy-processing roles for tiny mineral structures. It is origin-of-life basic science.

articles.title: A Rare, Genetic Respiratory Disorder is Discovered
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reports that variants in TMEM63B were identified as causing a rare genetic respiratory disorder. The summary suggests a gene-discovery and diagnosis-oriented advance. Specific phenotype details are limited in the provided record.

articles.title: A Natural Antifungal Mechanism is Revealed
articles.summary_3_sentences: This report describes a natural mechanism that counters Aspergillus fumigatus. The article is basic science focused on host-fungus interactions. The summary is short but clearly identifies an antifungal immunity finding.

articles.title: Scientists Reveal a Novel Layer of Gene Regulation in the Heart
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article reports a newly described layer of gene regulation in the heart involving adaptive molecular mechanisms. It appears to center on alternative splicing and cardiac response to changing demands. The finding is basic cardiovascular biology.

articles.title: Scientists are seriously asking if bees and ChatGPT are conscious
articles.summary_3_sentences: This piece reviews research on whether consciousness should be inferred from behavior alone in animals or AI systems. It argues for greater attention to internal mechanisms and concludes current AI is likely not conscious. The article is conceptual neuroscience and philosophy of mind rather than biomedical intervention research.

articles.title: Beluga whales keep switching mates and it may be saving their species
articles.summary_3_sentences: DNA analysis of Alaska belugas revealed a flexible mating system in which both sexes reproduce with different partners over time. The article suggests this may help maintain genetic diversity. It is an evolutionary ecology finding with conservation relevance.

articles.title: A hidden pollutant is changing how the world's forests breathe
articles.summary_3_sentences: A global analysis found that nitrogen pollution can either speed up or suppress forest soil respiration depending on ecosystem context. The results suggest hidden tipping points in carbon cycling and climate response. This is environmental systems science, not medical research.

articles.title: Indigenous Andeans Had a Genetic Advantage to Eating Potatoes
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reports that Indigenous Andean populations had a genetic adaptation linked to long-term potato consumption. It is a human evolutionary genetics story connecting diet and adaptation. The summary is brief but the theme is identifiable.

articles.title: How Zombie Immune Cells Drive Fatty Liver Disease
articles.summary_3_sentences: This report describes how senescent or "zombie" immune cells may contribute to fatty liver disease. The article focuses on cellular mechanisms and inflammatory pathways. The limited summary suggests a basic-to-translational disease biology finding.

articles.title: This common amino acid helped mice survive deadly inflammation
articles.summary_3_sentences: In mice, dietary methionine improved survival during severe infection and inflammatory stress by enhancing kidney filtration of inflammatory molecules. The study did not directly suppress immunity but altered systemic clearance. These are preclinical findings that may guide future metabolic approaches to critical illness.

articles.title: Omega-3 fish oil shows promise against type 2 diabetes
articles.summary_3_sentences: In diabetic rats, omega-3 supplementation improved blood sugar, cholesterol, and inflammatory measures while shifting immune cells toward a less inflammatory state. The article suggests metabolic benefit even without obesity. The findings are preclinical and not direct evidence for human treatment efficacy.

articles.title: The ocean's health may depend on a tiny microbe inside fish
articles.summary_3_sentences: Scientists found evidence that microbes in marine fish guts help produce calcium carbonate important to ocean chemistry. The finding revises assumptions that fish alone drive this process. It is a marine microbiology story with climate and ecosystem implications.

articles.title: Scientists discover inherited traits that break Mendel's Laws of genetics
articles.summary_3_sentences: A mouse study identified numerous epigenetic inheritance patterns that do not follow classic Mendelian rules, including a naturally occurring mammalian paramutation. The work suggests environmental effects may shape heredity more than traditionally appreciated. It is foundational genetics research.

articles.title: The secret to pigeons' incredible navigation was hiding in their liver
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers identified iron-containing immune cells in pigeon livers that may function as magnetic sensors for navigation. Birds lacking these cells had more trouble returning home under overcast conditions. The work bridges immunology, sensory biology, and animal behavior.

articles.title: This newly discovered raptor may have hunted like a giant heron
articles.summary_3_sentences: A newly described dinosaur from Patagonia appears to have been adapted for fish hunting with a flexible neck and specialized vertebrae. The article compares its feeding style to that of modern herons. It is a paleontology report outside direct medical science.

articles.title: Scientists say evolution may work differently than we thought
